topic: Marketplace interference exact-identification failure by augmenting-path certificate. Pre-anchor: closest published anchors are marketplace/platform experiment papers on two-sided interference and bipartite matching, plus Aronow-Samii/Savje exposure-mapping misspecification only as a distant comparator; closest banked proposals are pid_network_exposure_v1 and pid_network_exposure_aliasing_graphpair_v1, but those use graph exposure counts rather than a matching augmenting path.

open_problems.open_problem: Characterize a finite augmenting-path certificate showing that one-hop buyer exposure summaries do not exactly identify focal matching-policy estimands.

source_refs.claim: Two-sided platform experiments exhibit interference because an intervention applied to one market participant changes another participant through marketplace competition; design bias depends on market balance.
source_refs.author: Johari, Li, Liskovich, Weintraub

source_refs.claim: The exposure-mapping framework compresses global assignments into unit exposures under arbitrary but known interference.
source_refs.author: Aronow, Samii

source_refs.claim: Exposure mappings often should not simultaneously define effects and impose the full interference structure because correct specification is questionable.
source_refs.author: Savje

source_refs.excerpt: generic class statement for d-regular count-alias graph pairs

open_problems.why_unsolved: Marketplace experiment papers establish competition-driven interference and exposure-mapping papers explain the role and fragility of low-dimensional summaries, while prior CausalSmith reviewers explicitly asked for matching/cover certificates instead of another local graph-count alias. The missing object is a finite exact-identification failure certificate in which the unobserved matching geometry, not merely a neighborhood exposure count, separates the focal matching functional.
open_problems.why_nontrivial: The natural attempt to summarize each buyer by its one-hop seller exposure fails because marketplace interference papers model competition across market participants and the prior reviewer specifically requires a matching/cover certificate rather than another local exposure count.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A statement-level impossibility theorem giving two finite bipartite markets with identical focal one-hop summaries and own treatment but different focal match-probability estimands, with the alternating path b1-s1-b2-s2 as the minimal certificate.

open_problems.open_problem: Build sharp partial-identification bounds for marketplace estimands under misspecified low-dimensional exposure mappings while enforcing bipartite matching feasibility.

source_refs.claim: Derives sharp bounds for network causal effects under misspecified exposure mappings for canonical neighborhood summaries, thresholds, and truncated interference.
source_refs.author: Schroder, Oprescu, Feuerriegel, Kallus

source_refs.claim: Marketplace bias varies with demand-side or supply-side randomization and market balance, showing that two-sided competition is part of the estimand geometry.
source_refs.author: Johari, Li, Liskovich, Weintraub

source_refs.excerpt: The obstruction is joint: the same hidden edge must simultaneously determine R_i(G,A) and R_j(G,A)

open_problems.why_unsolved: Network exposure-mapping robustness gives sharp bounds for abstract misspecified summaries, and the prior network-exposure run isolates shared-completion compatibility as the obstacle. Neither source incorporates a bipartite matching constraint where feasible assignments and maximum matchings couple buyers and sellers globally, so the sharp set over matching-policy worlds remains uncharacterized.
open_problems.why_nontrivial: The natural attempt to import existing network partial-identification bounds fails because the cited bounds instantiate neighborhood summaries without shared matching feasibility, while the prior proposal flags joint completion as the missing constraint.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A sharp lower-and-upper-bound theorem over all potential-outcome and matching-policy worlds consistent with observed one-hop summaries, the assignment design, and finite bipartite matching feasibility.

open_problems.open_problem: Determine whether two-sided or multiple randomization designs identify matching-policy effects when the intervention changes the feasible matching rather than only outcomes inside fixed exposure cells.

source_refs.claim: Multiple randomization designs address marketplace settings where multiple populations interact strategically and yield main, direct, and spillover estimators under local interference.
source_refs.author: Masoero, Vijaykumar, Richardson, McQueen, Rosen, Burdick, Bajari, Imbens

source_refs.claim: Two-sided market experiments require randomization inference that respects buyer-seller structure; standard studentization is not universally valid under weak nulls.
source_refs.author: Liu, Shaikh, Toulis

source_refs.claim: Exact unbiased Horvitz-Thompson estimation is available in matching markets when the assignment and matching mechanism is simulable.
source_refs.author: Chen

open_problems.why_unsolved: Multiple-randomization and two-sided-inference papers develop estimators and tests for direct, spillover, total, or weak-null effects under structured randomized designs, while the matching-market mechanism paper relies on simulability of the assignment/matching rule. The open gap is whether design information alone can identify policy effects when treatment changes the feasible matching correspondence and not only the exposure label assigned to a fixed outcome unit.
open_problems.why_nontrivial: The natural attempt to combine two-sided randomization with Horvitz-Thompson weights fails because matching-market work requires the counterfactual mechanism to be simulable and multiple-randomization papers define direct and spillover cells rather than a changed feasible-matching map.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A design-based identification theorem or impossibility theorem stating whether observed two-sided randomization plus graph/matching data can distinguish augmenting-path counterfactuals for policy-induced matching changes.

open_problems.open_problem: Formalize the marketplace exposure-summary alias relation that determines when two matching mechanisms share the observed law but differ in the causal target.

source_refs.excerpt: Define the count-summary equivalence relation formally
a summary does not define a target unless the induced estimand/equivalence relation is specified
finite graph-pair exposure aliasing sounded concrete but lacked a hard theorem beyond the witness

open_problems.why_unsolved: The prior exposure-alias attempt failed because its summary-equivalence and induced-target notions were underdefined, even though the finite alias witness was correct. A marketplace version must say exactly which sigma-field or summary law the analyst observes and what it means for two matching mechanisms to be observationally aliased but target-separated.
open_problems.why_nontrivial: The natural attempt to present a hand-computed market pair fails because the prior reviewer says a summary does not define a target unless the induced estimand and equivalence relation are specified.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A formal alias relation over finite bipartite matching mechanisms plus a separation theorem showing that an augmenting-path certificate can change the focal estimand inside one alias class.

literature_map:
Johari, Li, Liskovich, and Weintraub (2022, Management Science) is the closest marketplace design anchor: interference biases two-sided platform experiments, and market balance determines whether customer, listing, or two-sided randomization is less biased.
Holtz, Felipe Lobel, Ruben Lobel, Liskovich, and Aral (2024, Management Science) empirically document Airbnb marketplace interference bias and assess cluster randomization, but the setting is not centralized matching-policy exact identification.
Masoero, Vijaykumar, Richardson, McQueen, Rosen, Burdick, Bajari, and Imbens (2026, JRSSB) formalize multiple randomization designs for interacting marketplace populations and direct/spillover estimands under local interference.
Liu, Shaikh, and Toulis (2025/2026, arXiv) develop randomization inference for two-sided market experiments and show standard studentization depends on the null formulation.
Sudijono, Lei, Masoero, Vijaykumar, Imbens, and McQueen (2026, arXiv) add regression adjustment for double-randomization marketplaces, reinforcing that classical one-sided formulas do not directly transfer.
Chen (2019, SSRN) is the matching-market causal-inference anchor: Horvitz-Thompson style exact estimation is possible when the mechanism is simulable, which is stronger than merely observing one-hop exposure summaries.
Doudchenko, Zhang, Drynkin, Airoldi, Mirrokni, and Pouget-Abadie (2020, arXiv) treat bipartite designs where treatment and outcome units differ, warning that analysis can introduce bias.
Aronow and Samii (2017, Annals of Applied Statistics) supply the foundational exposure-mapping framework for known interference.
Savje (2024, Biometrika) separates exposure definitions from assumptions and names misspecification as a core obstacle.
Schroder, Oprescu, Feuerriegel, and Kallus (2026, arXiv) give sharp partial-ID bounds for misspecified network exposure mappings, but not bipartite matching feasibility.
The scanned literature therefore covers marketplace competition, two-sided randomization, bipartite exposure designs, and exposure misspecification separately.
No scanned source gives a minimal augmenting-path exact-identification failure certificate for focal match probabilities under identical one-hop buyer exposure summaries.
prior_proposal_map:
Subagent A mined the adjacent banked qids pid_network_exposure_v1 and pid_network_exposure_aliasing_graphpair_v1 under doc/formalization/_bank.
pid_network_exposure_v1 supplied the strongest positive signal: shared-completion nonsharpness, strict cellwise-relaxation nonsharpness, and an unproved certificate family.
Its angle0_v4 reviewer explicitly prescribed K_i=1 matching/cover certificates and a small capacity witness, which cross-links to the current augmenting-path market object.
pid_network_exposure_aliasing_graphpair_v1 supplied the closest failed negative control: a finite C6/2K3 exposure-count alias was correct but too elementary and under-generalized.
Its reviewers asked for a formal summary-equivalence relation, a decision procedure for minimal exposure invariants, and a generic class statement rather than one finite pair.
No prior proposal found the present marketplace-specific exact-ID augmenting-path certificate; the reusable substrate is a network-completion/certificate problem transferred into matching geometry.
The appended study-pipeline gaps were LATE/Manski/interval-regression questions and did not materially anchor this marketplace-matching topic.
